title: Did Victorians Really Refuse to Smile
subtitle: 维多利亚人真的不笑吗？摄影史误读的真相

paragraphs.en: Victorians are often imagined as stern people who never smiled in photographs. Yet many surviving portraits show laughter and warmth, challenging that stereotype directly. These images narrow historical distance by revealing familiar human expressions.
paragraphs.zh: 维多利亚时代的人常被想象成一脸严肃、从不在照片中微笑的刻板形象。然而大量存世的肖像照却展现了笑意与温情，直接挑战了这一成见。这些图像通过展现熟悉的人类表情，拉近了我们与历史的距离。

paragraphs.en: Technology explains part of the story. Early cameras required long exposure, so neutral faces were practical because people had to hold still. By the late nineteenth century, faster devices made spontaneous smiles much easier to capture.
paragraphs.zh: 技术因素解释了其中一部分原因。早期相机需要长时间曝光，人们必须保持静止，因此中性表情更为实用。到了十九世纪后期，设备速度提升，抓拍自然的微笑就变得容易多了。

paragraphs.en: Social norms supplied another reason for restraint. In a period of limited dental care, showing teeth could signal poor hygiene or low status. Even respected writers warned that frozen grins might damage the dignity of a portrait.
paragraphs.zh: 社会规范则提供了另一重克制的理由。在牙科护理尚不普及的年代，露出牙齿可能被视为卫生不佳或社会地位低下的标志。就连一些备受尊敬的作家也告诫说，僵硬的咧嘴笑会损害肖像应有的尊严。
